_comment: Macaulay Library audio recordings for the 11 song notations Bailey transcribed. Each entry pairs a notation crop with a Macaulay asset that matches Bailey's specific phrase where possible. Source: each pick is from the curated Cornell All About Birds 'Sounds' page for the species (which lists Macaulay's editorially-selected recordings). Embedding terms are Macaulay's standard non-commercial allowance — no prior permission required for shellylynnx.github.io hosting. See 'Opera Glass v2' brainstorm in Notion for license context.
_embedPattern: Use Macaulay's official embed widget (the iframe code from each asset's 'Embed' button), not direct-linked MP3s. Lazy-load via IntersectionObserver to keep chapter pages fast.

embeds.selectionRationale: Cornell's primary featured 'song' recording; clean clear phrase typical of Bailey's day-song description.
embeds.caption: Modern recording of the Eastern Wood-Pewee's typical day song for comparison with Bailey's transcription.

embeds.selectionRationale: Captures the descending-glissando whistle phrase Bailey illustrated; second-listed song recording on Cornell ABA.
embeds.caption: Modern recording showing the descending whistle pattern Bailey notated as a 'U of sound.'

embeds.selectionRationale: Captures repeated three-pair phrase characteristic of the twilight song Bailey transcribed; matches the variant Wallace Craig later formally documented in 1926. If a more specifically-labeled 'twilight song' or 'dawn song' recording exists in the Macaulay catalog, prefer that.
embeds.caption: Modern recording showing the three-pair 'dear-ie' phrase Bailey notated as part of the Wood Pewee's twilight song. Wallace Craig documented this same song variant 37 years later in 'The Auk' (1926).

embeds.selectionRationale: Modern eBird-era 'Flight Call' recording (catalog ID > 100M, post-2020 era — typically clean field recordings without the older Macaulay narrator voice-overs). Onondaga NY — Bailey's New York territory. Exact behavior match for Bailey's 'dee-ree, dee-ee-ree' transcription, which is the goldfinch's undulating-flight call. Replaces 27018301 (older catalog) and earlier 539588 (Blue Jay in background).
embeds.caption: Modern recording of the American Goldfinch flight call, the 'dee-ree' Bailey transcribed.

embeds.selectionRationale: Cornell's primary 'song' recording; clean modern capture of the song mnemonized today as 'Old Sam Peabody, Peabody, Peabody' — Bailey heard the same phrase in 1889 as 'I-I-pea-bod-dy.'
embeds.caption: Modern recording of the White-throated Sparrow's song. Today's birders mnemonize it as 'Old Sam Peabody, Peabody, Peabody'; Bailey heard the same phrase in 1889 as 'I-I-pea-bod-dy.'

embeds.selectionRationale: 5.0-rated Macaulay 'Song' recording (no narrator); Cattaraugus, NY — close to Bailey's New York / Massachusetts territory. Replaces 107318 (older recording with human announcer voice on intro).
embeds.caption: Modern recording of the Ovenbird's escalating 'teach-er teach-er' song for comparison with Bailey's transcription.

embeds.selectionRationale: Modern eBird-era 'Song' recording (catalog ID > 100M, post-2020 era — typically clean field recordings without the older Macaulay narrator voice-overs), 4.83-rated, Ontario (Eastern subspecies, close to Bailey's New York / Massachusetts territory). Replaces 78304321 (older catalog) and earlier 66708 (narrator on intro).
embeds.caption: Modern recording of the White-Crowned Sparrow's whistled song for comparison with Bailey's 'whe-he-he-he-hee-hö' transcription.

embeds.selectionRationale: Cornell's primary 'song' recording; captures the explosive accented phrase Bailey transcribed as 'Te-ka-te-ka' ending with the accented 'teek.'
embeds.caption: Modern recording of the American Redstart's song, matching Bailey's 1889 transcription of 'Te-ka-te-ka-te-ka-te-ka-teek.'

embeds.selectionRationale: 5.0-rated modern eBird-era recording (catalog ID > 100M, post-2020 era — these are typically clean field recordings without the older Macaulay narrator voice-overs). Tompkins NY (Bailey territory). Replaces 140091 which still had a narrator.
embeds.caption: Modern recording of the Black-Throated Blue Warbler's buzzy song for comparison with Bailey's 'z-ie' transcription.

embeds.baileyPhrase: three-part main song with mid-phrase trills
embeds.selectionRationale: 5.0-rated modern eBird-era recording (catalog ID > 100M, post-2020 era — typically clean field recordings without the older Macaulay narrator voice-overs). Clinton NY (Bailey's Adirondack territory). Three-part song with the mid-phrase trill Bailey transcribed. Replaces 100856 which still had a narrator.
embeds.caption: Modern recording of the Hermit Thrush's three-part main song with mid-phrase trill — what Bailey transcribed.

embeds.selectionRationale: Alternate Cornell-listed recording showing song variation; captures the broken-form variant Bailey illustrated as 'ah re oo-oo.' If a more specifically broken-song recording surfaces in the Macaulay catalog, prefer that.
embeds.caption: Modern recording of a Hermit Thrush song variation, capturing the broken-form 'ah re oo-oo' phrase Bailey illustrated as a song variant.
